texts.label: text
texts.orig: Post combustion carbon capture (PCCC) washes the flue gas with a solvent that captures the CO2. This absorption process makes use of the reversible nature of the chemical reaction of an aqueous alkaline solvent (usually an amine) with an acid gas (CO2). Amine PCCC has some important drawbacks such as its high energy penalty and loss of solvent. A lot of effort is being put into the energy penalty issue and the continuous search for solvents that require less stripping heat (e.g. Zhao et al., 2010). Solvent is lost during the process due to: degradation, entrainment, vapourization and mechanical losses. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The study presents pioneering on-line emission data from a PCCC (post combustion carbon capture) pilot plant of both inorganic and organic components measured using an extractive FTIR (Fourier Transform Infra Red) system. A comparison with other emission measurements allowed concluding that the system is suitable as research tool for investigating the effect of PCCC operational settings on amine emissions. However, it also showed that sampling and analysing single organic components in flue gases is difficult and more research is needed on this topic. Linking the operational data of the plant with the on-line emission data identified two important operational settings that control the amine emissions from a PCCC plant to a large extent: (i) flue gas temperature at the top of the absorber (downstream of the washing section) and (ii) flue gas temperature difference over the washing section. A positive correlation between NH3 emission and the O2 content entering the pilot plant suggests that oxidative degradation occurs. The study emphasizes the importance of a well-designed washing section (if necessary, multi stage) which will be able to reduce emissions to very low levels keeping in mind the lessons learned in this study with respect to its operational settings. Therefore, the knowledge obtained within this research project will be of great use for the future post combustion carbon capture demo and full-scale installations.

All these studies were carried out under controlled laboratory conditions and analysis of the gas phase was only reported by Rochelle's team (Chi and Rochelle, 2002; Goff, 2005). However, in a real-life CO2 capture plant facility, the process is more complicated because the gaseous feedstock consists of a mixture of CO2, O2, CO, SO x , NO x , HCl as well as particulate matter (Strazisar et al., 2003). Acid gases like SO2, HCl, and NO2 react with the amine to form (i) heat-stable salts that reduce the CO2 absorption capacity of the solvent and/or (ii) degradation products that may be volatile and thus leave the process along with the flue gases. Oxygen is known to be the main driver for the oxidative degradation of amines (e.g. Chi and Rochelle, 2002). The effect of particulate matter in the flue gas on the emissions from amine based carbon capture installations has not been described in literature before. However, its concentration could be important since submicron (<1 𝛍 m) particulate matter may serve as nuclei for the formation of amine aerosols. The CO concentration is believed to have no effect on the carbon capture process and on the emissions. As a consequence, characterization of the gas phase is of great importance. The IEAGHG (2010) report states that activities in relation to this topic are limited and there is a clear need for cooperation and knowledge exchange between the different institutes working on the subject.

texts.orig: of acid gas impurities in power plant flue gas adversely affects the performance and cost. Only more recently, it has become evident that these impurities may lead to increased degradation (e.g. Thitakamol et al., 2007) of the amines and thus emissions of organic components together with the flue gas. It is clear that emissions of organic components must not only be monitored but also be reduced to a minimum since their release may cause environmental concerns. Only very little literature is available on these emissions and their impacts. The existing literature (e.g. Chi and Rochelle, 2002; Goff, 2005; Sexton and Rochelle, 2009; Nguyen et al., 2010; Freeman et al., 2010) describes the volatility and degradation of amines but under controlled laboratory conditions; no in situ degradation study was found. A valuable source of information on the environmental impacts of amine emissions was exchanged during a workshop held on 16th February of 2010 in Oslo resulting in the IEAGHG (2010) report. In this report, several studies report measured gaseous emissions but these are again mostly carried out in laboratory conditions. A few tests have been carried out in in situ conditions (at pilot plants) but in that case, offline measurements are usually carried out. Main conclusion from this workshop was that emissions measurement and evaluation tools require major focus and to do so, pilot plant tests using real flue gases and online measurements for chemical emissions are essential. Representative gaseous sampling and consequent accurate component identification using spectroscopic methods are both challenging mainly due to the water saturated conditions. This is true both for on-line and off-line (manual) monitoring systems.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The levels of some emissions measured in the Esbjerg pilot plant are relatively high. However, the washing section in the Esbjerg pilot plant (a pioneering PCCC installation) is relatively small and not designed for amine solvents of higher volatility than MEA. Furthermore, the Esbjerg pilot plant (and in fact of many pioneering pilot plants throughout Europe) has been designed primarily to demonstrate the PCCC concept on coal flue gas and to investigate energy saving options. Therefore the focus was on a reduction of the energy penalty and process optimization issues. Emissions were at that time not a key priority. This has however changed since then and in many newly build pilot plants, minimisation of emissions is often a focus area. Emissions are thus site-specific and to avoid the (mal-) use of the emission values, no absolute emission numbers are presented in this study. Moreover, the objectives of this research do not include presenting PCCC emission values as such but only aim at presenting, evaluating and using an on-line monitoring to research how emissions from PCCC can be reduced as a result of the pilot plant operational settings.

texts.orig: 2.1. Esbjerg pilot plant
texts.text: 2.1. Esbjerg pilot plant

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 1 presents an overall layout of the Esbjerg pilot plant and indicates the sampling locations of both the FTIR and manual measurements. CO2 absorption takes place in what is generally called an 'absorber' at temperatures that typically vary between 40 and 70 ◦ C. The flue gas then undergoes a water wash section to balance water in the system and to minimise any solvent droplets or solvent vapour carried over, and then leaves the absorber. The 'rich' solvent, which contains the chemically bound CO2, is then pumped to the top of a stripper (or regeneration vessel) through a heat exchanger. The stripping-off of the CO2 (or the regeneration of the chemical solvent) is carried out in the stripper at elevated temperatures (100-140 ◦ C) and pressures not very much higher than atmospheric pressure. Heat is supplied to the stripper to maintain the regeneration conditions. This leads to a thermal energy penalty as a result of heating up the solvent, providing the required desorption heat for removing the chemically bound CO2 and for steam production which acts as a stripping gas. The 'lean' solvent, containing far less CO2 is then pumped back to the absorber via the lean-rich heat exchanger and a cooler to bring it down to the absorber temperature level whilst recovering heat.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CO2 absorption pilot plant is installed at the Esbjerg power plant (West coast of Denmark) and is owned and operated by DONG Energy. The power plant is a 400 MW pulverised coal-fired power plant equipped with a cold sided electrostatic precipitator (ESP), a high dust selective catalytic reduction (SCR) to reduce the NO x and a wet limestone scrubber as flue gas desulphurisation unit (FGD). This flue gas cleaning installation ensures a saturated flue gas at around 47 ◦ C with less than 10 ppm SO2, less than 30 ppm NO x and less than 10 mg Nm -3 dust. The CO2 pilot plant was erected and commissioned in 2005. It takes on a slipstream of flue gas (5000 Nm 3 h -1 ) downstream of the SCR and FGD plants which corresponds to 0.5% of the power plant's flue gas flow. At an absorption degree of 90%, the CO2 production rate is 1000 kg/h (at 12% CO2). The CO2 absorber is equipped with a single washing section consisting of 3.0 m of structured packing. More information on the pilot plant's characteristics and operation can be found in Knudsen et al. (2007).

texts.orig: Sampling gaseous emissions from the top of the absorber is challenging because of the high humidity and low temperatures (water saturated at around 50 ◦ C). These conditions imply a potential risk of water condensation and thereby loosing water soluble components. Moreover, water is an interferent in many off and online gaseous measurement techniques and this is particularly true for Infra Red techniques as explained below. To avoid condensation, the entire gas sampling system is heated up to a temperature of 180 ◦ C, including the probe's insertion tube into the flue gas duct, filter inside the probe's head, sampling line and FTIR (Fourier Transform Infra Red) analyser itself. This implies that the actual measurements of the components inside the analyser takes place at 180 ◦ C avoiding the need for a water removal system prior to the measurement. texts.orig: The FTIR technique takes a snapshot of a rather large wavelength band inside the IR (Infra Red) region and then uses inverse optimization techniques to calculate which components and at which concentration make up the measured absorption spectrum.
texts.text: The FTIR technique takes a snapshot of a rather large wavelength band inside the IR (Infra Red) region and then uses inverse optimization techniques to calculate which components and at which concentration make up the measured absorption spectrum.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The FTIR technique uses absorption spectra (=reference spectra) of each of the components in its database to reconstruct the measured IR spectrum. This implies that the database must contain the reference spectra of the components that are expected and that need to be measured. The FTIR can measure simultaneously up to 50 components at the same time. Calibrating an FTIR for a specific component thus implies the saving of a new reference spectrum of this component in nitrogen and adding it to the database. For more details on FTIR spectroscopy, the reader is referred to any spectroscopic measurements textbook.

For the components for which the analyser has been TUV certified, the manufacturer states an uncertainty of 2% of the smallest certified range in nitrogen. Despite the fact that the FTIR has not been TUV certified for amines, a similar uncertainty is to be expected. The uncertainty will increase in complex matrixes where interference between components is high as is the case in amine based carbon capture installations (e.g. high water and CO2 concentrations as well as possible presence of organics components). Since water has a strong absorption throughout the IR spectrum, it is the most important interferent that needs to be taken into account. Neither detection limits nor quantification limits were available for AMP and PZ but as an example a MEA detection limit of 0.6 mg Nm -3 and quantification limit of 1.9 mg Nm -3 was received for our application. Similar values can be expected for AMP and PZ.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The FID measurement technique is a widely used and standardised technique (cf. EN12619, 1999) for measuring the Total Organic Carbon (TOC) content in flue gases. The analyser is usually calibrated using propane. Since not all organic components respond in the same manner to the flame ionisation, response factors have been determined for most of the organic groups. In literature, no response factor range for AMP and PZ as such were found but response factors for other amines (Sten, 2004) reveal a range between 0.67 (e.g. 1-amino-2-propanol), 0.7 (dimethylaminopropanol), . . . up to 1. This implies that 1 mg C Nm -3 measured by the FID (expressed as C propane) corresponds to only between 0.7 and 1 mg C Nm -3 . The FID (TESTA 1230) used in this research was set-up to measure in series with the FTIR during a short validation period of approximately 20 h. This means that the same flue gas sample is first analysed by the FTIR and consequently sent to the FID.

texts.orig: 2.3. Manual emission measurement campaign
texts.text: 2.3. Manual emission measurement campaign

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An accredited laboratory was called in at the Esbjerg pilot plant to perform an offline (or manual) sampling of ammonia and selected organic components at the top of the absorber. The compounds that were analysed for are: ammonia, AMP, PZ, acetaldehyde and formaldehyde (together with O2 and H2O for conversion to dry standard reference conditions). Acetaldehyde and formaldehyde are sampled in impingers filled with 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine (DNPH), whilst NH3 is sampled in impingers filled with dilute H2SO4. For both sampling trains, two impingers are installed in series to avoid breakthrough. The amines were sampled for in two different sampling trains: (i) an empty impinger (moisture trap) followed by two TD (thermal desorption) tubes and (ii) two impingers containing NIT (Naphthylisothiocyanate) which is referred to as the in-house method of the accredited laboratory for analysing the amines. All samplings were carried out at iso-kinetic conditions. The water content of the flue gas was also measured using a single catchpot in series with a pot of silicagel. Additionally, the oxygen concentration was continuously monitored by a paramagnetic O2 Automated Measuring System (AMS). During the manual sampling, the pilot plant was operated at steady state conditions throughout the entire sampling period with a solvent system of 3 M AMP and 1.8 M PZ.

texts.orig: Fig. 3 presents the comparison between the sum of AMP and PZ as measured by the FTIR but expressed as mg C Nm -3 and the TOC as measured by the FID and expressed in the same units. A good linear correlation ( R 2 = 0.92) is observed between both measurements and the slope of 0.8 corresponds to a FID response factor of 0.8 for the mix of AMP and PZ. This is within the range 0.7 and 1 as found in literature. This comparison allows concluding that the FTIR measurements of AMP and PZ are plausible and no other organic compounds are present in the flue gas at large concentrations. This is in-line with the FTIR readings for all other organic components in its library which were found to be smaller than 1 mg Nm -3 .

texts.orig: The AMP concentrations measured by both manual methods show a large difference: the AMP found on the TD is 100 times lower than the concentration found when using the in-house method. One could think of several reasons why the AMP was not found on the TD tubes: (i) AMP is not reaching the tube, (ii) AMP is not being absorbed on the TD tubes, (ii) amines are not being extracted from the tubes for the laboratory measurement (e.g. gas chromatography), . . . It is unclear which reason is most likely but we have experienced that problems related to the sampling of water soluble gaseous emissions are common. Additionally, the possible presence of amines in the form of aerosols may also increase the difficulties with respect to a correct sampling.

texts.orig: The discrepancy between the on-line measurements (FTIR) and the manual measurement of a factor three is most likely due to sampling artefacts. Further investigation is needed to identify which artefact is the cause but very likely it is related to losses due to condensation. Since both on-line techniques measure at 180 ◦ C throughout the entire sampling system, chances of condensation losses are minimal. The possible presence of amines in the form of aerosols as well as the fact that the manual sampling and the FTIR sampling locations were different may have an influence on the results. Aerosols are evaporated inside the FTIR sampling lines and system the entire system is kept at 180 ◦ C whilst they may not be (totally) captured by the manual sampling methods. Differences in concentrations because of the different sampling locations (within the same flue gas duct, see Fig. 1) of FTIR and manual measurements should be small since both sampling sites were carefully chosen (far away from bends, ventilators, . . . ) to minimise the risk of an inhomogeneous concentration distribution across the flue gas duct. This study serves as motivation to increase efforts with respect to the development of onas well as off-line sampling and analytical procedures for measuring organics in water saturated flue gas.

Fig. 4 shows that this resulted in a doubling of the NH3 emissions and MEA emissions increase by a factor 20 or more. The increase in the flue gas temperature can be noted as the sudden increase in the water content which increased from around 12-25 vol% as indicated in Fig. 4. Problems related to the temperature readings do not allow plotting the temperature values in Fig. 4 but since we are in water saturated conditions, the water content is directly related to the flue gas temperature. The increase in water content corresponds to an increase in the flue gas temperature from 45-47 ◦ C up to 65 ◦ C.

This shows that the oxidative degradation of MEA is an process that needs to be considered when interpreting ammonia emissions. This has been shown in laboratory tests before (Chi and Rochelle, 2002). It is clear that for pilot plant conditions, the oxygen content alone does not suffice for predicting the NH3 emissions. Other processes such as temperature profiles inside the absorber and the stripper, chemical composition of flue gas entering pilot plant, chemistry of the solvent (e.g. presence of metal ions that may catalyse oxidative MEA degradation), . . . can influence the MEA degradation rate This is reflected in the rather large variation in NH3 emissions when plotted against oxygen content (see Fig. 5).

texts.orig: 3.4.1. Test A-decreasing the flue gas outlet temperature out of the washing section (50 ◦ C → 40 ◦ C)
texts.text: 3.4.1. Test A-decreasing the flue gas outlet temperature out of the washing section (50 ◦ C → 40 ◦ C)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To investigate the effect of the flue gas outlet temperature (out of the washing section), it is reduced by 10 ◦ C through an increase of the cooling water flow rate into the cooler of the washing section. All other operating parameters of the pilot plant were kept constant during the test. As shown in Fig. 6, this reduction leads to a significant decrease of the AMP by a factor 4. For the PZ emissions, the measured concentrations were only slightly above the FTIR detection limits and the data are therefore not plotted although they do show a similar behaviour. The problem of this test is that during this period, the pilot plant was operated without a closed water balance, i.e. water build up within the plant. The reason is that the temperature of the flue gas leaving the absorber is lower than at the inlet, which means that water is condensed and starts building up in the solvent loop. To compensate for the net condensation of water from the flue gas, condensate from the stripper condenser was drained during the test. This is not an optimal way of operating a PCCC plant because the condensate is not pure water and thus has to undergo waste water treatment. On top of the water balance issue, an increase in cooling power is needed to decrease the temperature of the washing section increasing the energy requirement of the PCCC pilot.

texts.orig: In this test, the lean solvent inlet temperature is reduced from 40 to 30 ◦ C. All other operating parameters of the pilot plant were kept constant during the test. As shown in Fig. 8, reducing the lean solvent inlet temperature to the absorber reduces the flue gas temperature reaching the washing section. As the temperature out of the washing section is dictated by the inlet temperature of the flue gas to the absorber (because of the water balance issue) this is more or less constant during the test. This implies that the difference between the flue gas temperature entering the washing section and the flue gas temperature leaving the washing section becomes small and even close to zero. This means that little or no water is condensed from the flue gas and thus no replacement of the washing water occurs. In other words the amine concentration builds up in the washing section, reducing the efficiency of the washing. In Table 3, the amine contents of washing water before and after the step change in lean are shown. It is clearly seen that the applied operating mode causes significant build-up of amines in the washing loop. texts.orig: The same observation is also clearly illustrated in Fig. 9 in which the decrease in AMP concentrations is plotted against the temperature difference between in and out of washing section. The low AMP emissions at a temperature difference of only 2-3 ◦ C originate very likely from a short transient period. During this period, more AMP is dissolved into the washing water until the new equilibrium is reached and the emissions start to rise. An important conclusion from this test is that condensing water in the washing section or addition of fresh make-up water is crucial for the reduction of emissions. In the absence of fresh water addition, the controlling operational parameter that determines the amount of water condensation is the temperature difference between the flue gas reaching the washing section and the flue gas leaving the washing section. This difference needs to be sufficiently large to ensure the condensation of water and thus an efficient washing of the flue gas.
texts.text: The same observation is also clearly illustrated in Fig. 9 in which the decrease in AMP concentrations is plotted against the temperature difference between in and out of washing section. The low AMP emissions at a temperature difference of only 2-3 ◦ C originate very likely from a short transient period. During this period, more AMP is dissolved into the washing water until the new equilibrium is reached and the emissions start to rise. An important conclusion from this test is that condensing water in the washing section or addition of fresh make-up water is crucial for the reduction of emissions. In the absence of fresh water addition, the controlling operational parameter that determines the amount of water condensation is the temperature difference between the flue gas reaching the washing section and the flue gas leaving the washing section. This difference needs to be sufficiently large to ensure the condensation of water and thus an efficient washing of the flue gas.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.4.3. Test C-washing water flow rate increase (10 → 20 m 3 h -1 )
texts.text: 3.4.3. Test C-washing water flow rate increase (10 → 20 m 3 h -1 )

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The washing water flow rate was increased in a single step from 10 to 20 m 3 h -1 . All other operating parameters of the pilot plant were kept constant during the test. No make-up water was added to the wash section during the test. As indicated in Fig. 10, this results in a slight and temporary decrease in the flue gas temperature leaving the washing section (because of the sudden increase in washing water flow) and thus in a slight and temporary decrease of AMP emissions. However, shortly after the decrease, the AMP emissions reach the same level as before the flow rate increase. This allows concluding that increasing the washing water flow rate without an increased cooling capacity did not affect the emissions. This indicates that the wetting of the packing, i.e. the gas/liquid contact, in the washing section is sufficient at a liquid loading of 10 m 3 h -1 . The typical applied washing water flow during testing is 15 m 3 h -1 .

texts.orig: 3.5. AMP-PZ campaign (one month continuous operation)
texts.text: 3.5. AMP-PZ campaign (one month continuous operation)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paragraph discusses the second part of the AMP-PZ campaign period which corresponds to a period of continuous operation of nearly one month during which all operational settings were kept constant. Fig. 11 presents the AMP and PZ emissions during this one month continuous operation. It is clear that for the AMP/PZ solvent combination, emissions of AMP are dominating indicating a high volatility of the AMP, i.e. higher than PZ and MEA. This is in accordance with expectations based on the physical properties of the components (Nguyen et al., 2010). All other organics present in the database of the FTIR (formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, MEA and MDEA) as well ammonia were not detected at levels above their detection limits (so <1-3 mg Nm -3 ).

texts.orig: 3.5.1. Influence of the CO2 content of the flue gas
texts.text: 3.5.1. Influence of the CO2 content of the flue gas

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An interesting inverse correlation is found between the CO2 content in the flue gas reaching the pilot plant as a result of changing operating conditions of the power plant and the AMP emissions under constant operating conditions of the pilot plant. This is shown in Fig. 12 which reveals a trend that high CO2 concentrations reaching the pilot plant lead to low AMP emissions and vice versa during a weekend operation of the power plant (i.e. varying loads).
texts.text: An interesting inverse correlation is found between the CO2 content in the flue gas reaching the pilot plant as a result of changing operating conditions of the power plant and the AMP emissions under constant operating conditions of the pilot plant. This is shown in Fig. 12 which reveals a trend that high CO2 concentrations reaching the pilot plant lead to low AMP emissions and vice versa during a weekend operation of the power plant (i.e. varying loads).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The reason for this observation is explained in Fig. 13. It shows that there is a positive correlation between the CO2 content and the temperature difference between the bottom and the top of the washing section. The reason for this correlation is the fact that the CO2 absorption is an exothermal process and therefore more absorption heat is released into the process when CO2 concentrations are high. Keeping the temperature of the flue gas from the washing constant implies that the temperature difference between bottom and top of absorber increases with increasing CO2 concentration. Similarly to what was observed above with respect to the lean solvent temperature, increasing this temperature difference increases the efficiency of the washing section and thus decreases the AMP emissions. Therefore high CO2 content concur with large temperature differences over the washing section which leads to low AMP emissions (see Fig. 13). There may however also be another effect: the higher the CO2 content coming in, the less 'free amine' is present in the solution. This indicates a lower vapour pressure of the amines and thus may also help in reducing the emissions.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The study presents pioneering on-line emission measurement data from a PCCC pilot plant. The emissions are monitored using an extractive system consisting of an FTIR analyser for measuring inorganic and organic components and a Zirconium analyser measuring the O2 concentration. A manual measurement campaign as well as a Total Organic Carbon measurement was carried out by an accredited laboratory. These campaigns allowed concluding that the FTIR measurements can be used for investigating the effect of PCCC operational settings on amine emissions. However, it also showed that sampling and analysing single organic components in flue gases is difficult and more research is needed on this topic.
texts.text: The study presents pioneering on-line emission measurement data from a PCCC pilot plant. The emissions are monitored using an extractive system consisting of an FTIR analyser for measuring inorganic and organic components and a Zirconium analyser measuring the O2 concentration. A manual measurement campaign as well as a Total Organic Carbon measurement was carried out by an accredited laboratory. These campaigns allowed concluding that the FTIR measurements can be used for investigating the effect of PCCC operational settings on amine emissions. However, it also showed that sampling and analysing single organic components in flue gases is difficult and more research is needed on this topic.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Linking the operational data of the plant with the on-line emission data identified two important operational settings that control the amine emissions from a PCCC plant to a large extent: (i) flue gas temperature at the top of the absorber (downstream of the washing section) and (ii) flue gas temperature difference over the washing section. As a result of this latter, a negative correlation is found between the CO2 entering the plant and the amine emissions. A positive correlation between NH3 and the O2 content entering the pilot plant suggests that oxidative degradation occurs when using MEA as a solvent.

As a result of this latter, a negative correlation is found between the CO2 entering the plant and the amine emissions. A positive correlation between NH3 and the O2 content entering the pilot plant suggests that oxidative degradation occurs when using MEA as a solvent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The level of some of the emissions from this pilot plant experiment are high. However, the washing section in the Esbjerg pilot plant is relatively small and was not specifically designed for volatile amine emissions. A well designed washing section (if necessary, multi stage) will be able to reduce emissions to very low levels keeping in mind the lessons learned in this study with respect to its operational settings. Therefore, the knowledge obtained within this research project will be of great use for the future post combustion carbon capture demo and full-scale installations.
